QF.id: XIII Trading, Clearing, Settlement and Risk Management_60

question.text: In physically settled bond futures (interest rate derivatives), what is the primary purpose of the conversion factor?

answer.text: To equalize the value of different deliverable bonds so that they can be delivered against the same futures contract
To convert the futures price from one currency to another
To adjust the contract size based on market volatility
To calculate the stamp duty payable on delivery

explanation.text: **The Logic:** Since multiple bonds with different coupons and maturities can be delivered against a single bond futures contract, the conversion factor adjusts the futures settlement price to account for the coupon and remaining maturity differences. This makes delivery of any eligible bond theoretically equivalent. **The Trap:** Currency conversion is irrelevant — these are INR-denominated contracts. Volatility-based adjustment relates to margins, not conversion factors. Stamp duty calculation is a separate administrative matter. **Cross-Exam Context:** Series IV (Interest Rate Derivatives) covers conversion factors and the cheapest-to-deliver (CTD) concept in detail. This concept is unique to interest rate derivatives — equity and currency derivatives do not use conversion factors. **Distributor's Pro-Tip:** Understanding conversion factors helps explain why the CTD bond trades at a premium or discount to the theoretical futures price — it is the bond whose delivery is most advantageous for the short position holder.
